summary: Graham argues that programming languages evolve like species, and that the most durable languages will be those with small, clean cores and a bias toward programmer convenience over machine efficiency as hardware grows faster. He predicts that future languages will separate semantics from implementation details, allowing late optimization while keeping early versions easy to write. Designing toward a “hundred‑year” language is a useful target because it highlights what choices will keep languages on the main evolutionary branches.
key_points: Programming languages evolve along main branches and dead ends; choosing languages near the main branch is a practical heuristic.
The core axioms of a language matter most; fewer, cleaner primitives increase long-term survival and flexibility.
As hardware improves, wasting machine cycles to save programmer time becomes the right tradeoff; inefficiency in code is less harmful than inefficiency in humans.
Separate program meaning from implementation details so optimization is optional and late, not baked into semantics.
Language design should be guided by imagining the least-work program and aiming for brevity, independent of current constraints.
money_quotes: I have a hunch that the main branches of the evolutionary tree pass through the languages that have the smallest, cleanest cores.
I can already tell you what's going to happen to all those extra cycles that faster hardware is going to give us in the next hundred years.  They're nearly all going to be wasted.
Inefficient software isn't gross.  What's gross is a language that makes programmers do needless work.
The right way to solve that problem, I think, is to separate the meaning of a program from the implementation details.
When you're working on language design, I think it is good to have such a target and to keep it consciously in mind.
key_insight: The best long-term bet in language design is a minimal, clean core that prioritizes programmer time and defers efficiency to optional optimizations.
